name: Online grocery delivery
summary: Online grocery ordering and delivery (Instacart, Walmart+, Amazon Fresh, Kroger, regional chains).

evidence.claim: FTC 2024 complaint against Instacart-style platforms finding in-store vs in-app prices differ by 8-15% without disclosure, plus service fees, delivery fees, and optional tips layered on top.
Consumer Reports 2023 grocery delivery markup study documenting average 20-25% total price premium for delivery vs in-store pickup, after all fees.
USDA Food Safety and Inspection Service guidance on cold-chain handling of delivered perishables (<41°F for ≤2 hours delivery).

criteria.name: price_markup_pct
criteria.notes: Total price difference from in-store baseline, INCLUDING fees + tip (E1, E2).

criteria.name: cold_chain_compliance
criteria.notes: Insulated packaging + delivery time ≤2 hrs for perishables (E3).

confabulationPatterns.reality: Service fees (5-10%), tips (suggested 15-20%), and price markups on individual items (8-15%) apply even when delivery is 'free' (E1). Total premium is often 20-25%.
confabulationPatterns.checkPrompt: Does the 'free' claim include service fees, markups, and expected tips?

confabulationPatterns.reality: Instacart and Amazon Fresh routinely mark up items 8-15% vs in-store prices for the same retailer (E1); only a few chains (Walmart+, direct Kroger app) offer price parity.
confabulationPatterns.checkPrompt: Is the service owned by the retailer (Walmart/Kroger/Target) or third-party (Instacart)? Third-party marks up.

confabulationPatterns.reality: Break-even for a $99/yr membership requires 30+ orders/yr; casual users (<1 order/month) rarely save money after membership fee.
confabulationPatterns.checkPrompt: User's actual order frequency — if <12/year, membership is likely not cost-effective.

compatibilityQuestions: Order frequency per month? Determines whether a membership subscription pays off.
Delivery vs pickup? Pickup avoids delivery fees and typical markup.
